section.title: The Lord's Supper
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from head coverings to a graver failure at the very table meant to unite the church. Their gatherings do more harm than good: the well fed shame the poor, and so what they eat is not really the Lord's Supper at all. Into this disorder Paul hands on the words of institution he received from the Lord, and calls each believer to examine himself and discern the body, lest the meal of grace become a meal of judgment.
section.thesis: The Lord's Supper proclaims Christ's death for us until He comes, so a church that shames the poor at that table has forgotten the cross; each believer must examine himself and discern the body, receiving the meal as the sign of the new covenant in Christ's blood, not as an occasion for division and judgment.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the rebuke, their divisions turn the supper into something that is not the Lord's Supper; the tradition received from the Lord, this is My body, the new covenant in My blood, proclaim His death until He comes; and the examination, discern the body, or you eat and drink judgment.

verses.text: In the following instructions I have no praise to offer, because your gatherings do more harm than good.
First of all, I hear that when you come together as a church, there are divisions among you, and in part I believe it.
And indeed, there must be differences among you to show which of you are approved.
Now then, when you come together, it is not the Lord’s Supper you eat.
For as you eat, each of you goes ahead without sharing his meal. While one remains hungry, another gets drunk.
Don’t you have your own homes in which to eat and drink? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing? What can I say to you? Shall I praise you for this? No, I will not!

units.label: The tradition received from the Lord
units.note: this is My body, the new covenant in My blood

verses.text: For I received from the Lord what I also passed on to you: The Lord Jesus, on the night He was betrayed, took bread,
and when He had given thanks, He broke it and said, “This is My {{soma|body}}, which is for you; do this in {{anamnesis|remembrance}} of Me.”
In the same way, after supper He took the cup, saying, “This cup is the new {{diatheke|covenant}} in My blood; do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of Me.”
For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until He comes.

units.label: Examine and discern the body
units.note: or you eat and drink judgment

verses.text: Therefore, whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of sinning against the body and blood of the Lord.
Each one must {{dokimazo|examine}} himself before he eats of the bread and drinks of the cup.
For anyone who eats and drinks without {{diakrino|recognizing}} the body eats and drinks judgment on himself.
That is why many among you are weak and sick, and a number of you have fallen asleep.
Now if we judged ourselves properly, we would not come under judgment.
But when we are judged by the Lord, we are being disciplined so that we will not be condemned with the world.
So, my brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.
If anyone is hungry, he should eat at home, so that when you come together it will not result in judgment. And when I come, I will give instructions about the remaining matters.

soma.gloss: Body. At the table Jesus calls the bread His body, given for us, and Paul uses the same word for sinning against the body and blood of the Lord and for failing to discern the body. It carries a double weight in this passage: the crucified body of Christ, and the church which is His body, so that despising the poor is treated as an offense against the Lord Himself.

anamnesis.gloss: Remembrance, a calling to mind that makes a past event present and active. Do this in remembrance of Me is more than nostalgia; it is a covenant memorial in which the church proclaims and lays hold of Christ's finished death, echoing the way Israel remembered the Passover deliverance.

diatheke.gloss: Covenant, a binding arrangement God graciously establishes. The cup is the new covenant in Christ's blood, fulfilling the promise of Jeremiah 31 of a covenant written on the heart with sins forgiven. The word points beyond a shared meal to the whole new relationship purchased at the cross.

dokimazo.gloss: To examine, test, and approve after scrutiny. Each one must examine himself before eating, not to earn a place at the table but to come honestly, repentant, and in love toward the body, so that the meal is received rightly rather than carelessly.

diakrino.gloss: To distinguish, discern, judge rightly between things. To eat without recognizing the body is to fail to discern what the meal is and who the church is, treating the Supper as common food and the poor brother as nothing; such careless eating brings judgment rather than blessing.

basic.context: The Corinthians came together for a shared meal that included the Lord's Supper, but their gatherings had become a display of the same divisions that ran through the whole letter. The rich ate their fill and even got drunk while the poor went hungry and were shamed. Paul says that when they eat like this it is not really the Lord's Supper at all. He reminds them of the words Jesus spoke on the night He was betrayed, and warns that to eat carelessly, without discerning the body, is to eat and drink judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is correction of a specific abuse, not a full theology of the sacrament, though it teaches us much. Paul grounds his rebuke in the tradition he received from the Lord, the words of institution, and applies it pastorally: examine yourself, wait for one another, and if you are only hungry, eat at home. The abiding truths are the meaning of the Supper and the call to receive it worthily.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus calls the bread His soma, His body, given for us, and the same word returns when Paul warns against sinning against the body and failing to discern the body. The Supper is done in anamnesis, remembrance, a living memorial that proclaims His death. The cup is the new diatheke, covenant, in His blood. So each believer must dokimazo, examine himself, and diakrino, discern the body, rather than eat carelessly.
basic.typology: The Supper fulfills the Passover: as Israel remembered its rescue from Egypt with a meal, the church remembers a greater rescue, for Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed. The new covenant in His blood fulfills Jeremiah's promise of a covenant with sins forgiven, sealed not with the blood of bulls but with the blood of the Lord.
basic.christ: Christ stands at the center of the table. It is His body given and His blood poured out that the bread and cup proclaim. The Supper looks back to His death for us on the cross and forward to His return, for we eat and drink until He comes. He is both the host who gives the meal and the sacrifice the meal proclaims.
basic.god: God is not mocked at His own table. He disciplines a careless church, even with weakness, sickness, and death, yet He does so as a Father, so that His people will not be condemned with the world. His judgment here is corrective mercy, aimed at bringing the church back to love and to the cross.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 22 records the same words of institution at the Last Supper. Exodus 12 gives the Passover the Supper fulfills. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood. 1 Corinthians 10 says the cup and bread are a participation in the blood and body of Christ.
basic.oneStory: From the Passover in Egypt to the new covenant promised by the prophets, God's rescue has always been marked by a meal. At the cross the true Lamb is slain, and the church now proclaims His death in bread and cup until He comes again to share the feast of the kingdom. The Lord's Supper is a hinge between what Christ has done and what He will yet do.

context.literary: Part of a long section on worship and church order, 1 Corinthians 11 to 14. After correcting conduct in prayer and prophecy, Paul turns to the assembly's central meal. The passage moves from rebuke, verses 17 to 22, to the received tradition, verses 23 to 26, to application, verses 27 to 34, framed at start and finish by the phrase come together.
context.historical: Early church gatherings often joined a common meal, sometimes called the love feast, with the Lord's Supper. In a society sharply divided by wealth and status, the well off could arrive early with ample food while poorer members and slaves came later to nothing. Corinth's factionalism had invaded even this sacred meal, turning it into a display of social contempt.
context.canonical: This is the earliest written account of the words of institution, predating the Gospels, received by Paul and passed on as fixed tradition. It stands alongside Matthew 26, Mark 14, and Luke 22, and connects the Supper to the Passover of Exodus and the new covenant of Jeremiah 31, gathering the Bible's meal and covenant themes onto Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront a shameful abuse of the shared meal, to re-anchor the church in the Lord's own words about the Supper, and to call each believer to self-examination and love for the body so that the meal is a proclamation of Christ's death rather than a cause of judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account of Corinthian abuse and its consequences is descriptive; the words of institution and the call to examine oneself, discern the body, and wait for one another are prescriptive. The discipline of weakness and death describes what happened at Corinth and warns us, but is not promised as the automatic result in every case.

debates.question: What does it mean to eat without discerning the body, verse 29?

views.label: Discerning the church as Christ's body
views.case: In context the sin is shaming the poor, so failing to discern the body means failing to recognize fellow believers as the one body of Christ and treating the meal as ordinary food; the cure is love and waiting for one another.

views.label: Discerning the significance of Christ's body and blood
views.case: The immediate phrase concerns the bread and cup, so discerning the body means grasping the weight of the Lord's death that the elements proclaim, and coming with reverence rather than carelessly.

debates.bearing: The two readings reinforce each other: to honor Christ's body at the table is to honor His people at the table, and both call for reverent, loving participation rather than proud self-indulgence.

debates.question: What is the unworthy manner that makes one guilty, verse 27?

views.label: A manner of eating, not a level of worthiness
views.case: Paul rebukes how they eat, with division, drunkenness, and contempt for the poor; unworthy manner describes careless, loveless participation, not a demand that one be sinless before coming.

views.label: A warning against presumptuous or impenitent eating
views.case: To eat while cherishing division or sin is to sin against the very body and blood proclaimed, so the warning calls for repentance and examination before approaching the table.

debates.bearing: Either way the remedy is examination and repentance, not staying away; the table is for repentant sinners who come in love, not for the flawless.

study.originalLanguages: The words of institution carry the freight of the passage. The bread is Christ's soma, His body given for us, and Paul deliberately reuses body when he speaks of sinning against the body and blood and of discerning the body, binding the crucified body of Christ to the gathered body of the church. The meal is kept in anamnesis, remembrance, a covenant memorial that makes the past deliverance present and proclaims the Lord's death until He comes. The cup is the new diatheke, covenant, in His blood, the fulfillment of Jeremiah's promise. So the summons is to dokimazo, to examine oneself, and to diakrino, to discern the body, distinguishing this holy meal and these fellow members from common food and casual company.

typology.fulfillment: The Supper fulfills Israel's Passover; Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, and the church now remembers a greater deliverance from sin and death in bread and cup.
The cup is the new covenant in Christ's blood, fulfilling the promise of a covenant with sins forgiven and God's law written on the heart, sealed by the blood of the Lord rather than of animals.

typology.type: The blood of the covenant at Sinai
typology.fulfillment: Moses sealed the old covenant by sprinkling the blood of the covenant, Exodus 24; Christ inaugurates the new and better covenant by His own blood poured out for the forgiveness of sins.

study.christ: The whole passage turns on Christ. It is His body, broken and given for us, that the bread proclaims, and His blood, poured out to seal the new covenant, that the cup declares. The Supper is a proclamation of the Lord's death, held between two comings: it looks back to the cross where He gave Himself for sinners, and forward to the day He returns to share the marriage feast. He is the true Passover Lamb sacrificed for His people, the mediator of the new covenant, and the risen Lord whose table this is. To eat rightly is to feed by faith on Him and to love the members He has joined to Himself.
study.god: God takes His table with utter seriousness. He will not be honored by a church that despises the poor, and He disciplines careless communicants, even with weakness, sickness, and death. Yet His judgment on His own people is fatherly discipline, not condemnation; He chastens so that they will not be condemned with the world. Here is a God who is both holy and gracious, who guards the meaning of the cross and lovingly corrects His children back to it.

crossReferences.note: The Last Supper account of the words of institution, this is My body and the new covenant in My blood, which Paul received and passed on.
The Passover meal that the Lord's Supper fulfills, a remembrance meal marking God's deliverance of His people.
The promise of a new covenant with sins forgiven, sealed in Christ's blood and declared over the cup.
The cup and the bread are a participation in the blood and body of Christ, and the one loaf makes the many one body.
Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, grounding the Supper in the true Passover.

study.oneStory: God's saving story is punctuated by meals: the Passover in Egypt, the covenant meal at Sinai, the promised feast of the kingdom. At the cross the true Lamb is slain and a new covenant is sealed in His blood. The Lord's Supper stands between His death and His return, a memorial that proclaims what He has done and a foretaste of the feast still to come. A church that shames the poor at this table has lost the thread of the whole story; a church that discerns the body keeps it.

quiz.q: Why did Paul say the Corinthians' gatherings did more harm than good?

options.text: Their divisions meant the rich ate their fill while the poor were shamed and went hungry.
options.note: Verses 21 and 22: they humiliated those who had nothing.

options.text: They were using the wrong bread and cup for the meal.
options.note: The problem was not the elements but the loveless, divisive way they ate.

options.text: They had stopped meeting together altogether.
options.note: They did come together; the failure was how they came together.

options.text: They were persecuted by outsiders during the meal.
options.note: The harm came from within, from their own contempt for the poor.

quiz.q: What does the cup represent according to the words of institution?

options.text: The new covenant in Christ's blood, poured out for us.
options.note: Verse 25: this cup is the new covenant in My blood.

options.text: A pledge of the believer's own effort and devotion.
options.note: It is grounded in Christ's blood, not our devotion, though it calls for faith.

options.text: Merely a symbol of fellowship among members.
options.note: It does bind believers together, but its heart is Christ's blood and the covenant.

options.text: The wisdom of God given to the mature.
options.note: That belongs to earlier chapters; the cup proclaims the new covenant in His blood.

quiz.q: What does the Supper proclaim, and for how long?

options.text: The Lord's death, until He comes again.
options.note: Verse 26 sets the Supper between the cross and the return of Christ.

options.text: The Lord's resurrection, until the end of the age.
options.note: The resurrection is our hope, but Paul says the meal proclaims His death until He comes.

options.text: The unity of the local church, for the present generation.
options.note: Unity matters, but the meal proclaims Christ's death, not merely our unity.

options.text: The forgiveness of a single past sin, once for all.
options.note: It proclaims His atoning death continually until He returns.

quiz.q: What is a believer to do before eating and drinking?

options.text: Examine himself and discern the body, coming in repentance and love.
options.note: Verses 28 and 29 call for self-examination and discerning the body.

options.text: Prove himself worthy by being free of all sin.
options.note: The table is for repentant sinners; the call is to examine, not to be sinless.

options.text: Wait to be judged first by the church leaders.
options.note: Paul calls for self-judgment, not clearance by leaders, though love for the body matters.

options.text: Nothing; the meal works regardless of the heart.
options.note: Careless eating brings judgment; the heart and the body must be discerned.

quiz.q: How should we understand the weakness, sickness, and deaths among them?

options.text: As the Lord's fatherly discipline, so they would not be condemned with the world.
options.note: Verse 32: being judged by the Lord is discipline, not condemnation.

options.text: As proof that God had abandoned the whole church.
options.note: It was corrective discipline of His own people, not abandonment.

options.text: As random misfortune with no connection to the Supper.
options.note: Paul directly links it to their abuse of the table, as discipline.

options.text: As final condemnation from which there is no return.
options.note: The point is the opposite: discipline now so they are not condemned later.

questions: The Corinthians turned a meal of grace into a display of status. Where might pride or partiality be quietly shaping how your church, or your table, treats those with less?
The Supper proclaims the Lord's death until He comes. How does holding both the cross behind us and Christ's return before us shape the way you take communion?
Paul says to examine yourself before you eat. What would honest self-examination look like for you the next time you come to the Lord's table?
To discern the body is to see fellow believers as Christ's own. Who in your congregation is easy to overlook, and how could you honor them as part of the body?
God disciplined the church as a Father so they would not be condemned with the world. How does seeing hardship as possible fatherly correction change the way you respond to it?

translationNotes.note: In remembrance of Me translates anamnesis, a covenant memorial rather than mere recollection; the meal proclaims and applies Christ's finished death, echoing how Israel remembered the Passover deliverance.
The new covenant in My blood renders diatheke, covenant, tying the cup to Jeremiah 31 and the blood of the covenant at Sinai; the Supper seals the new and better covenant in Christ's blood.

Without recognizing the body translates diakrino, to discern or distinguish; the phrase can point to discerning the meaning of Christ's body and blood, to discerning the church as His body, or to both together.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 11:17-34; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas, with wording unchanged.
